what_it_does: Compares three self-hosted chat UI platforms (Open WebUI, LobeChat/LobeHub, LibreChat) as a front-end for a multi-agent fabric that emits server-initiated content, and recommends a fork path because none supports the required unsolicited push natively.
observations: None of the three platforms provides fabric-owned server-to-UI push into custom panels without a fork.
The comparison is organized around four stated priorities: inbound-message surfacing, external call-out, mobile usability, and fork/extend cleanliness.
A comparison table covers frontend/backend stacks, data stores, push support, call-out options, mobile, fork cleanliness, tools, auth, MCP support, license, and maturity.
The file recommends forking LibreChat, lists Open WebUI as a conditional alternative, and argues LobeChat is unsuitable as a fork base due to license and UI-fork friction.
License terms are treated as a decision factor: Open WebUI has a BSD-3 plus branding clause, LobeHub requires a commercial license for derivatives, and LibreChat is MIT.
An explicit 'honest gap' section states that the inbound surfacing layer must be built regardless of platform choice.
standouts: The discriminator is unsolicited server-initiated push, which all three platforms lack.
License encumbrance is treated as a first-class selection criterion alongside code architecture.
The recommendation is conditional on whether the fabric's output is autonomous push or request-scoped streaming.
conventions: Markdown report with title, restated use-case, comparison table, weighted scoring, recommendation, and caveat sections.
Uses priority-order weighting and a primary/alternative/not-recommended structure.
concerns: Claims sources are 'live June-2026' inside a research document, which may be a dating convention or error.
Spot-check claims are asserted but the primary sources are not shown in this file.
The recommendation is prescriptive even though the file acknowledges all options require custom development.
notes: A decision synthesis that selects a fork base by mapping license terms and code architecture to the need to build a custom server-push layer.
questions: What is the intended fabric surfacing pattern: unsolicited push or request-scoped streaming reply?
What are the exact thresholds and costs for the Open WebUI branding clause and LobeHub commercial license in this context?
How was the '3–5 files' estimate for adding a push channel to LibreChat derived?
Which specific primary sources were spot-checked for the license and MCP claims?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — it states it is a synthesis and comparison, then delivers one.
apparent_themes: License risk as an architectural constraint.
Fork cost versus out-of-the-box feature fit.
Conditional recommendation based on surfacing pattern.
Explicit acknowledgment of remaining build work.
intention_signals: To guide platform selection by reframing the question from 'which product has the feature' to 'which product is the most suitable base for a required custom push layer'.
novelty: The explicit framing that the core requirement (unsolicited server-push into custom panels) is unsupported by all candidates, so the comparison becomes a fork-base selection rather than a feature checklist.
contribution: A conditional decision framework that links license terms, code architecture, and the fabric's surfacing pattern to a suggested fork base and an alternative, plus an explicit list of gaps that remain in every option.
summary_for_embedding: A head-to-head comparison of Open WebUI, LobeChat/LobeHub, and LibreChat as self-hosted front-ends for a multi-agent fabric, concluding that none supports unsolicited server-push natively and recommending a fork path conditional on license terms and surfacing pattern.
intention_for_embedding: To synthesize prior platform analyses into a selection recommendation for a fabric front-end that requires custom inbound-message surfacing.
